file_name: Abbes and Sellami - 2019 - Deep Neural Network for Fuzzy Automatic Melanoma Diagnosis.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a Deep Neural Network (DNN) classifier for the automatic diagnosis of melanoma from skin lesion images. The method involves feature extraction based on the ABCD rule, fuzzy modeling using Fuzzy C-Means (FCM) to create a Bag-of-Words representation, and classification via a DNN to determine lesion severity (benign vs. malignant).

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a new Computer-Aided Diagnosis (CAD) system using a Deep Neural Network and Fuzzy C-Means for melanoma diagnosis. It details a specific methodology (feature extraction, fuzzy modeling, classification), describes an experimental dataset of 206 images, and reports original performance metrics (sensitivity 90.1%, accuracy 87.5%) compared against prior studies. Although published in conference proceedings (VISIGRAPP 2019), it contains original results and is not a review.

title: Deep Neural Network for Fuzzy Automatic Melanoma Diagnosis
journal: Proceedings of the 14th International Joint Conference on Computer Vision, Imaging and Computer Graphics Theory and Applications (VISIGRAPP 2019)
brief_summary: This study proposes a Computer-Aided Diagnosis (CAD) system for automatic melanoma detection using skin lesion images captured by standard optical cameras. The method extracts features based on the ABCD rule (Asymmetry, Border, Color, and Differential structures) and applies Fuzzy C-Means clustering to model the Bag-of-Words representation, addressing image imprecision. These fuzzy membership degrees are then fed into a Deep Neural Network classifier to distinguish between benign and malignant lesions. The system was evaluated on a public dataset of 206 skin lesion images sourced from DermQuest and Dermatology Information System. Experimental results demonstrated a sensitivity of 90.1% and an accuracy of 87.5%, outperforming several comparative state-of-the-art methods in terms of sensitivity and accuracy.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target class for diagnosis. The dataset contains 119 melanoma images. The study aims to distinguish melanoma from benign lesions.

The comparator class for the binary classification task. The dataset contains 87 benign lesion images. The term 'benign common nevi' is used in the introduction to describe the classes identified by the DNN.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesion severity (benign vs. malignant melanoma)
all_task_types.details: The paper proposes a Deep Neural Network (DNN) classifier to distinguish between benign lesions and malignant melanoma. The model uses fuzzy Bag-of-Words features as input and outputs discrete diagnostic categories (benign or malignant). Performance is evaluated using sensitivity (90.1%) and accuracy (87.5%).

datasets_used.name: Combined DermQuest and Dermatology Information System (DIS) Dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dataset of skin lesion images obtained using standard optical cameras in varying environmental conditions. The images were extracted from two online public databases: DermQuest and Dermatology Information System (DIS).

use.training: Used for training the Deep Neural Network classifier after feature extraction and fuzzy modeling.
use.validation: Not explicitly reported as a separate split; the dataset was used for experimental evaluation.
use.testing: Used for experimental evaluation to calculate sensitivity (90.1%) and accuracy (87.5%).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using sensitivity (90.1%), specificity (84.4%), and accuracy (87.5%) on a dataset of 206 images, and compared descriptively to other studies in Table 2.

Combined DermQuest and Dermatology Infor.notes: The proposed system achieved the best accuracy and sensitivity compared to the listed state-of-the-art methods. The dataset consisted of 206 images (119 melanoma, 87 benign lesions).

architecture_used.pretraining: None reported; the DNN was trained on fuzzy Bag-of-Words membership degrees derived from the specific dataset, not on raw image data or pre-trained weights.
architecture_used.input_size: Input vector size corresponds to the number of Bag-of-Words (BoW) membership degrees (features extracted from Asymmetry, Border, Color, and Differential structures), but exact dimensionality is not explicitly stated.

layers.input_layer: Nodes representing membership degrees of the lesion to each BoW
layers.hidden_layers: Two hidden layers included to represent possible interactions among input variables
layers.output_layer: Nodes representing class identity (Benign vs. Melanoma)

classification_or_output_head.details: Output layer represents the identity of classes: benign and melanoma.

preprocessing_techniques: Median filter application for noise removal
Feature normalization (rescaling features to interval [0,1] using mean and standard deviation)
conclusions: The proposed Computer-Aided Diagnosis (CAD) system, which combines Fuzzy C-Means (FCM) for Bag-of-Words modeling with a Deep Neural Network (DNN) classifier, effectively addresses the imprecision and vagueness inherent in skin lesion images captured by standard optical cameras.

The system achieved a sensitivity of 90.1% and an accuracy of 87.5% on a public dataset of 206 skin lesion images (119 melanoma, 87 benign lesions), demonstrating that fuzzification of feature modeling yields good diagnostic results.

The proposed approach outperformed several state-of-the-art comparative methods (Celebi and Zornberg, 2014; Codella et al., 2017; Lopez et al., 2017; Yu et al., 2017; Quang et al., 2017) in terms of both sensitivity and accuracy.

The study highlights that many existing CAD systems suffer from low sensitivity due to high false negative rates (misclassifying melanoma as benign), and the proposed method mitigates this by accounting for data ambiguity through fuzzy modeling.

The use of standard optical cameras rather than expensive dermatoscopes makes the proposed method more accessible, particularly in regions where dermatoscopes are limited or costly, although it requires robust handling of lower image quality and contrast.

The dataset used in this study was balanced regarding melanoma samples (more samples of melanoma than used in some state-of-the-art works), which may have contributed to the improved sensitivity compared to methods using unbalanced datasets.
important_remarks: The study uses a very small dataset of only 206 images (119 melanoma, 87 benign), which limits the statistical power and generalizability of the results.

The dataset was sourced from DermQuest and Dermatology Information System (DIS) using standard optical cameras, not dermatoscopes; images have poor contrast and less saturated colors, introducing significant imprecision and vagueness.

The dataset is balanced (more melanoma samples than typical state-of-the-art works which often have 80% benign/20% melanoma splits), which likely contributes to the high sensitivity (90.1%) compared to other methods.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals were reported; performance was evaluated using sensitivity, specificity, and accuracy on the single dataset.

The Deep Neural Network architecture details are sparse: it uses two hidden layers with hyperbolic tangent sigmoid activation and gradient descent, but exact layer dimensions, number of neurons, learning rate, or regularization techniques are not explicitly reported.

The input to the DNN consists of fuzzy Bag-of-Words membership degrees derived from features (Asymmetry, Border, Color, Differential structures) extracted via manual segmentation and Fuzzy C-Means, rather than raw pixel data, which may limit the model's ability to learn complex spatial features compared to end-to-end CNNs.

Comparisons with state-of-the-art methods (Celebi and Zornberg, Codella et al., Lopez et al., Yu et al., Quang et al.) are descriptive only; these comparative studies used dermoscopy images and different datasets, making direct performance comparison potentially biased due to domain shift and dataset differences.
Manual segmentation was required for preprocessing, which introduces potential human bias and limits scalability compared to fully automated segmentation methods.
The paper does not report external validation results; the model was only evaluated on the specific combined DermQuest/DIS dataset.
